---
title: "How to Use This Guide"
---

This is a field guide to the craft of narrative and game design, for anyone who
builds experiences that other people play: tabletop roleplaying games,
interactive fiction, escape rooms, puzzle hunts, live and immersive events, and
the board and video games beside them. It treats design as a set of functions
rather than a set of media. A clue behaves much the same whether it is hidden in
a novel, a room, or a save file, so the guide is organised by what a mechanism
*does*, not by the medium it happens to appear in. Each article carries the media
it touches as tags, so a single idea can be followed across all of them.

## The six families

The articles are grouped into six families, each a distinct design discipline.

- **Structure & Pacing**: how an experience is shaped over time, from branching maps to the rhythm of a single session.
- **Puzzles, Clues & Information**: the craft of fair challenge and the controlled release of what a player comes to know.
- **Story, Character & Voice**: the fiction itself, and the craft of character, voice, prose and genre that shapes it.
- **Players & Social Dynamics**: the people at the table and the trust between them, held together by clear roles and safety tools.
- **Space, Props & Materiality**: the physical and digital objects of play, from a sealed letter to a lock to a screen.
- **Systems & Mechanics**: the rules engine, from dice and economies to the loops that keep a game worth replaying.

## How an article is built

Every article opens with a one-line *Silhouette* that names the mechanism in a
sentence. **The Mechanism** then explains how it works, and **Where this fits**
places it among its neighbours, with links to follow. Two kinds of call-out
punctuate the text: a **Specimen** points to a real work where the mechanism can
be seen in the wild, and a **Hazard** marks the way it most often fails.

Each family is introduced by a **primer** that sketches the terrain before the
articles go deep, and closes with a **Works & References** page crediting the
games, films, books and essays the family draws on.

## Finding your way in

You can read a family from its primer onward, or follow the cross-links between
articles wherever one mechanism leans on another. The **Exemplars** gazetteer
profiles real works, a game or a hunt or a show, and links each back to the
mechanisms it demonstrates. The **Glossary & Index** is an A-to-Z of every
mechanism and the other names it goes by.

::: {.content-hidden when-format="typst"}
The **Keys** route you to articles by what you need rather than by name: by the
*effect* you want to create, the *problem* you are trying to solve, the
*components* you have to hand, or by *genre*, *medium* and *format*.
:::

## Two editions

This guide is both a printed book and a web wiki, built from one source. The
wiki is the complete edition. The print edition is curated, leaving some
web-native material (the Keys, the wider research trails) to the wiki alone,
where links and search make it useful. Where the two differ, the wiki has more.
